title: Greece Buys Israeli 'Achilles Shield' for €3.5B, Korea-US Launch MASGA Shipbuilding Center, Lockheed Hits Record $230B Backlog
subhead: Greece approves a €3.5B Israeli air defense dome, South Korea and the US open a $150B shipbuilding partnership center, Lockheed Martin posts record earnings driven by missile demand, and Russia loses another Su-57 — its entire fifth-generation fleet under strain.

sections.heading: Greece Approves €3.5B 'Achilles Shield' With Israeli Systems
sections.body:
Greece's KYSEA defense council approved a €3.5 billion ($4 billion) purchase of Israeli anti-missile, anti-aircraft, and anti-drone systems on Thursday, establishing a layered air defense network dubbed 'Achilles Shield.' Around €3 billion of a broader €4.2 billion spending package will go toward Israeli-made systems, according to a Greek defense ministry source who spoke to AFP. The deal also includes Embraer C-390 Millennium transport aircraft, Israeli Heron drones, Apache helicopter missiles, British-made Victa stealth watercraft, and frigate sonar systems. Greek defense firms will contribute €700 million to the dome project domestically.

Defense Minister Nikos Dendias confirmed the approvals on social media, calling them a transformation of Greek military capabilities. Greece now spends over 3% of GDP on defense, one of only four European NATO members to do so, driven by decades of tensions with Turkey. Prime Minister Mitsotakis has called this the 'most drastic' defense overhaul in modern Greek history within a broader €25 billion, 12-year modernization program. Greece has also signed for four Belharra frigates and 24 Rafale fighters from France, plus 20 F-35s from the US. Notably, the State Department confirmed that Turkey has still not met legal requirements for readmission to the F-35 program, adding another layer of strategic asymmetry between the two NATO rivals.
sections.whyItMatters: Greece is building one of the most comprehensive integrated air defense networks in Europe, using Israeli combat-proven systems — a procurement choice directly shaped by the Ukraine war's lessons about drone and missile threats. The deal reinforces Israel's position as a top-tier defense exporter to NATO states and deepens the strategic gap between Greek and Turkish air capabilities.

sections.heading: Korea-US Launch $150B Shipbuilding Partnership Center in Washington
sections.body:
South Korea and the United States formally opened the Korea-US Shipbuilding Partnership Center (KUSPC) in Washington on Thursday, advancing the $150 billion MASGA ('Make American Shipbuilding Great Again') initiative committed under last year's bilateral trade deal. Industry Minister Kim Jung-kwan called the center a symbol of South Korea's 'strong commitment' to US shipbuilding revitalization, while Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick stressed that the center must produce tangible shipbuilding results — not just meetings.

Korean companies are already developing projects to acquire, expand, and modernize shipyards across the US, Kim said. Financial institutions will coordinate to support the MASGA investment pipeline, which targets naval vessels and LNG carriers built in America. Lutnick pledged to 'clear regulatory roadblocks' for Korean investors. New US Ambassador to South Korea Michelle Steel called the partnership an expansion of the bilateral alliance into 'a new, incredibly important territory.' The center will promote joint R&D, technology sharing, and workforce development for thousands of skilled shipyard workers.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the most significant US shipbuilding industrial partnership in decades, leveraging Korean world-class commercial shipyard expertise to address America's chronic naval construction capacity gap. If MASGA delivers, it could reshape the global shipbuilding order and reduce US dependence on Asian yards for both commercial and naval hulls.

sections.heading: Lockheed Martin Posts Record $230B Backlog, Raises 2026 Guidance
sections.body:
Lockheed Martin reported strong Q2 2026 results with $20.1 billion in quarterly sales and raised its full-year sales guidance to $79.75B–$81.75B. The company's backlog hit a record $230 billion, driven by surging missile demand and sustained F-35 production at a rate of 156 aircraft per year. The THAAD $35 billion win from earlier this quarter contributed significantly to the order book.

Lockheed Martin's venture arm separately announced it will invest $100 million in European defense firms, targeting quantum computing, sensing, autonomy, and advanced manufacturing. The fund signals Lockheed's strategic bet on European defense technology startups at a time when European governments are dramatically increasing procurement budgets. Meanwhile, Lockheed also unveiled the PAC-3 ACE interceptor — a next-generation missile defense solution for the Patriot system, positioned as a more affordable option for allied missile defense.

The Q2 earnings paint a picture of a defense prime operating at full capacity with war-driven demand showing no sign of easing. Free cash flow for the year is projected above $7 billion.
sections.whyItMatters: A $230 billion backlog at Lockheed means the defense industrial base is sold out for years — but it also means delivery timelines will remain long. The $100M European venture investment is a notable shift: American primes are not just selling to Europe, they're buying into it, positioning for a prolonged spending supercycle.

sections.heading: Russia's Su-57 Fleet Takes Another Hit as Stealth Fighter Crashes Near Moscow
sections.body:
A Russian Su-57 fifth-generation fighter jet crashed during a training flight on Thursday near Zvenigorod in the Odintsovo district outside Moscow. The pilot ejected safely. Eyewitnesses reported the aircraft exploded after ejection, according to Russian outlet Astra. Russian authorities have not disclosed the cause.

The Su-57 is Russia's most advanced combat aircraft, designed for air superiority and precision strike missions with claimed stealth and advanced avionics. However, Russia is believed to operate only about 30 Su-57s — far short of its goal of 76 additional aircraft by 2027. The type has rarely been used in combat over Ukraine, likely due to Moscow's fear of losing one to air defenses. This is at least the third confirmed Su-57 loss: two were previously hit on the ground in June 2024 and April 2026. Recent imagery also showed the type flying with external R-73 missiles on wing pylons rather than internal bays, a configuration that compromises its already-questioned stealth characteristics.
sections.whyItMatters: Every Su-57 loss matters when the total fleet is around 30 aircraft. The crash underscores that Russia's fifth-generation program remains troubled — not just in combat survivability, but in basic operational reliability. With serial production chronically behind schedule, each attrition event further widens the gap between Russian fighter capability claims and reality.

sections.heading: UK Pours £708M Into Tempest, Unveils Brontanax Drone Wingman
sections.body:
The UK Ministry of Defence awarded £708 million ($946M) to extend BAE Systems' contract for the Future Combat Air System (FCAS), part of the Global Combat Air Programme (GCAP) with Italy and Japan. The funding covers digital engineering, AI, and advanced manufacturing including augmented reality, additive manufacturing, and robotics. A flying demonstrator is scheduled for next year, with service entry targeted for 2035. The UK government has now committed £8.6 billion over four years to GCAP on top of over £5 billion already spent.

At Farnborough, BAE Systems also unveiled 'Brontanax' — an unmanned Collaborative Combat Aircraft (CCA) prototype, similar in size to a Hawk trainer jet, designed for electronic warfare and precision strike alongside crewed fighters. BAE expects it in service before the end of the decade. Separately, the UK is investing £300 million ($402M) in a 'Storm Fighter' autonomous combat aircraft program.

Canada announced it will join GCAP as an Observer, gaining access to aircraft information and capabilities, with a potential future purchase under consideration. The MoD also awarded a £20 million ($27M) contract to Lockheed Martin UK for Project Bowline, a hypersonic target vehicle under the AUKUS partnership, with a live flight demonstration planned for 2027 at the Hebrides range.
sections.whyItMatters: The UK is now the most committed GCAP partner in absolute spending, and the Brontanax reveal shows it's not just building a manned fighter — it's developing an entire air combat system. Canada's observer status adds another potential customer and production base to a program that needs economies of scale to compete with the F-35.

sections.heading: House Passes $95B Iran War Funding as Conflict Cost Hits $37.5B
sections.body:
The House adopted a $95 billion Republican budget package on Wednesday, with the bulk earmarked for the Iran war — $60 billion for military operations and $12 billion for other national security needs, plus $10 billion for farm aid and $10 billion for voting law changes. The vote was near party-line. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth told the Senate Appropriations Committee on Tuesday that the Iran war has cost $37.5 billion so far, up from earlier estimates, with 17 American service members killed and over 100 wounded since early July.

Hegseth defended the request by saying 'without these funds, we face critical shortfalls,' but faced fierce pushback. Sen. Patty Murray warned of 'another forever war,' while Sen. Jeanne Shaheen questioned why the Pentagon needs more money when roughly half of last year's $150 billion defense supplemental remains unspent. Joint Chiefs Chairman Gen. Dan Caine conceded he 'can't answer the question on what it will cost, because the enemy has a vote.' The House separately passed a resolution to halt military action in Iran, but the Senate rejected a similar measure.

The $1.15 trillion NDAA also passed the House this week, including $750 million for security ties with Israel. Meanwhile, the war's economic fallout deepened as Houthi strikes on two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea pushed Brent crude above $100/barrel for the first time in two months, and US munitions stockpiles continue to deplete under the sustained strike tempo.
sections.whyItMatters: The $37.5B cost figure — and climbing — is now a political liability. The war is consuming defense resources at a rate that strains both the budget and the arsenal, while congressional support is softening. The Houthi tanker strikes add a maritime economic dimension that makes the war's costs tangible at the gas pump.

sections.heading: Raytheon-Ukraine Patriot Co-Production, Thales Record Orders, Germany Defense Fund
sections.body:
Raytheon-Ukraine Patriot Deal: President Zelensky announced on July 23 that Ukraine and Raytheon signed a co-production agreement for Patriot interceptor missiles. The landmark deal would establish domestic production of the interceptors Ukraine desperately needs, though details on timeline, technology transfer, and licensing remain to be worked out. It follows Ukraine's broader push to become a defense exporter rather than just an aid recipient.

Thales H1 2026: French defense giant Thales reported a 21% jump in orders to €12.47 billion for the first half, with defense orders up 28% to €7.35 billion. Sales grew 6.7% to €10.95 billion, and adjusted operating profit rose 9.9% to €1.37 billion. Middle East demand was a major driver. The company took a €450 million charge from Germany's cancellation of the F126 frigate program but confirmed its raised 2026 targets: organic sales growth of 6-7% and an operating margin of 12.6-12.8%.

Germany Defense Investment Fund: Germany established a state-backed investment fund for defense technologies, targeting AI, drone systems, autonomous vehicles, cybersecurity, and satellite communications. Unlike conventional grants, the fund takes equity stakes in startups — including firms like Helsing (AI defense systems) and ARX Robotics (autonomous vehicles). KfW Bank and private venture firms will co-invest, with the program extending beyond 2030. Germany's venture investment in 2025 was only €7.2 billion, far below the UK and US, highlighting the capital gap the fund aims to fill.

Canada Drone Procurement Portal: Canada opened a pre-qualification marketplace for domestic drone suppliers on Thursday, organized into five streams: uncrewed/counter-uncrewed systems, data/EW/cyber, systems integrators, testing and evaluation, and innovation/experimentation. Six priority categories include low-cost tactical ISR drones, complex-terrain UGVs, modular explosive payloads, counter-drone interceptors, maritime autonomous systems, and sovereign deep precision strike. The portal is the most tangible output yet of Canada's Defence Industrial Strategy, which mandates 70% of military acquisitions go to domestic firms.
sections.whyItMatters: A convergence of industrial moves across multiple allies shows the defense spending supercycle is now generating real industrial capacity — not just orders. The Raytheon-Ukraine deal, if it works, shifts Ukraine from aid recipient to co-producer. Thales' numbers confirm the order boom has reached the balance sheet. Germany's equity fund and Canada's drone portal represent new models of state-backed defense investment that go beyond traditional procurement.

bottomLine: July 24 marks a day when the defense industrial supercycle showed up on balance sheets and in procurement offices worldwide. Greece's €3.5B 'Achilles Shield' purchase from Israel creates a new integrated air defense architecture in southeastern Europe. South Korea and the US formalized a $150B shipbuilding partnership that could transform American naval construction capacity. Lockheed Martin's record $230B backlog and Thales' 28% defense order surge confirm that the spending boom is now hitting revenue. The UK committed nearly £1B to its future fighter and drone programs, while Canada opened its drone procurement doors to domestic industry. Russia's Su-57 crash is a reminder that fleet numbers still matter — and Moscow's are thin. Meanwhile, the Iran war's $37.5B and rising price tag is reshaping US defense politics, with munitions stockpiles under strain and congressional patience wearing thin.
sources: Yonhap, Le Monde/AFP, Reuters, AP, Kyiv Post, The Register, sUAS News, AeroTime, Seeking Alpha, RaillyNews, Euractiv, Daily Sabah, Breaking Defense, Bloomberg, Defense Express, The War Zone, Canada Department of National Defence, UK Ministry of Defence, Thales SA, Lockheed Martin, US House of Representatives, US Senate Appropriations Committee, USNews, The Hill, FT, Aviation Week
